title: Add Podman configuration to prevent starting containers with --privileged flag
body:
### Feature request description

Need to prevent user from running privileged containers when root file-system is read-only through bind mounts and chroot. This is required for hardening the system. More details can be provided in a private channel.

### Suggest potential solution

When containers.conf::privileged (https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/pkg/config/containers.conf#L293) attribute is set to "never", then fail container create/start/run commands when used --privileged flag.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

A clear and concise description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered.

### Additional context

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

comments.body:
What exactly are you trying to achieve with that? A user on its own cannot prevent itself from using the capabilities it is given.
Blocking this via config file means any user can just unset that setting again and still use it

Aside from that --privileged is just a super option that disables many security settings, any user can still use --cap-add, --security-opt seccomp=unconfined, etc...

> This is needed in a hardened environment where there is only one user that can elevate to root shell. The elevated shell presents the root file-system in RO mode. What that means is that this user cannot make configuration changes in the host root file-system. Hence, resetting the containers.conf file setting is not possible by this user.

No any user can set any config file of their own even on a read only fs, first there are env vars that a user can just point anywhere where they can write to (because podman needs a writable location it means the user running podman must always have a writable location).
But even besides that even with fully ro the user can just podman unshare and overmount /etc/containers with an empty tmpfs.

> I understand that other Linux capabilities can be added. But that won't be exactly same as the --privileged option. If you need more details, we can jump into a call due to sensitivity of the requirement.

It will be the same because as I said privileged is a superset of various other options. Blocking just this one flag like that cannot work security wise.

> No any user can set any config file of their own even on a read only fs

The podman commands are invoked using `--url <socket>` option which internally turns on the `remote` option. Does a custom config file apply in this case?

> The podman commands are invoked using --url <socket> option which internally turns on the remote option. Does a custom config file apply in this case?

So far it is a bit of a mess because the file is being read both client side and server side with some options only working client side while others server side. Most container settings should be read server side, I think privileged should be one of that. Given what a mess that part of the code is I would be very careful to not promise any kind of guarantees about the exact behaviours.

Now if there is a clear clear client/server separation then I agree there is in theory a chance to lock things down for the user where they would be unable to set certain options via the API. In theory anyone could have a MITM proxy which parses the json and rejects non conformant requests. I would like to avoid such things in podman, so far security wise API access == full control over the user who runs the service. That helps a lot to deal with most security issues, as they are none in that context. If we have a untrusted barrier in the API layer it means any HTTP parsing, memory allocation DOS, etc... will suddenly become a real issue.

> so far security wise API access == full control over the user who runs the service.

I would agree to this if there was a server driven access control (RBAC) mechanism in Podman. There needs to be a mechanism wherein the server can dictate and enforce certain policies.

Right now, the ask is to provide a server enforced way that when podman container create/start/run command with `--privileged` flag is rejected when called over socket/remote.
